title: Tabernaemontana decaisnei
intro: Tabernaemontana decaisnei, also known as Tabernaemontana or Five-angled Tabernaemontana, is a species of flowering plant in the family Apocynaceae, native to Southeast Asia. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, which include anti-inflammatory, antimalarial, and antioxidant effects, providing relief from fever, pain, and digestive issues. The bioactive constituents of Tabernaemontana decaisnei include alkaloids such as tabernaemontanine, dihydrocorynantheine, and corynantheine, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, Tabernaemontana decaisnei is often prepared as a decoction, tincture, or capsule, and is used to treat various health conditions, including fever, malaria, and digestive issues.
taxonomy: Tabernaemontana decaisnei is classified as a species within the Apocynaceae family. This family of plants is commonly known as the dogbane or oleander family. The Apocynaceae family belongs to the order Gentianales. The order Gentianales is further classified within the clade asterids in the class Magnoliopsida. This classification reflects the evolutionary relationships and shared characteristics of Tabernaemontana decaisnei with other plants in the Apocynaceae family.
uses: Tabernaemontana decaisnei, a plant native to Southeast Asia, is reported to have various medicinal uses. It is used in traditional medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and respiratory issues. The plant's leaves and roots are believed to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. In some cultures, it is also used to treat skin conditions and wounds. Further research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of Tabernaemontana decaisnei for medicinal purposes.

The plant has been traditionally used in Southeast Asian and Pacific Island cultures to treat a range of health issues, from fever and respiratory infections to digestive problems and wounds.
